channel.title: Nature
channel.description: Nature is the foremost international weekly scientific journal in the world and is the flagship journal for Nature Portfolio. It publishes the finest peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature publishes landmark papers, award winning news, leading comment and expert opinion on important, topical scientific news and events that enable readers to share the latest discoveries in science and evolve the discussion amongst the global scientific community.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: #ScientistAtWork 2026: Nature seeks striking photographs that capture researchers at work
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 27 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01266-5

Competition winners receive a cash prize and will see their images featured in Nature.
item.dc:title: #ScientistAtWork 2026: Nature seeks striking photographs that capture researchers at work

Alexia Austin

item.title: Field re-entrant superconductivity in Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10547-y

Field re-entrant superconductivity in Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates
item.dc:title: Field re-entrant superconductivity in Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10547-y Field re-entrant superconductivity in Eu-doped infinite-layer nickelates

item.title: Caspase 5c amplifies Wnt via APC cleavage to promote intestinal homeostasis
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10343-8

CASP5 expression is restricted to the human intestinal epithelium, and the CASP5C isoform has a key role in promoting Wnt signalling, which is required for epithelial homeostasis, through binding to dishevelled and cleavage of APC to regulate β-catenin turnover.
item.dc:title: Caspase 5c amplifies Wnt via APC cleavage to promote intestinal homeostasis
item.dc:creator: Baosen Jia, Yuhua Shi, Yourae Hong, et al.; last authors: Sabine Tejpar, J. Magarian Blander

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10343-8 CASP5 expression is restricted to the human intestinal epithelium, and the CASP5C isoform has a key role in promoting Wnt signalling, which is required for epithelial homeostasis, through binding to dishevelled and cleavage of APC to regulate β-catenin turnover.

item.title: Multicentre gene therapy for OTOF-related deafness followed up to 2.5 years
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10393-y

In a multicentre trial of AAV1-hOTOF gene therapy involving 42 participants aged 0.8–32.3 years with autosomal recessive deafness 9, treatment demonstrated safety, tolerability, and improved hearing and speech perception, with age and distortion product otoacoustic emissions associated with treatment outcomes.
item.dc:title: Multicentre gene therapy for OTOF-related deafness followed up to 2.5 years
item.dc:creator: Luoying Jiang, Xiaoting Cheng, Jun Lv, et al.; last authors: Zheng-Yi Chen, Yilai Shu

item.title: Electronic origin of reorganization energy in interfacial electron transfer
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10311-2

By tuning graphene’s electronic density of states, the study shows electrode electronic structure—not just the electrolyte—dominates reorganization energy and thus controls outer-sphere electron-transfer rates at solid–liquid interfaces.
item.dc:title: Electronic origin of reorganization energy in interfacial electron transfer
item.dc:creator: Sonal Maroo, Leonardo Coello Escalante, Yizhe Wang, et al.; last authors: David T. Limmer, D. Kwabena Bediako
Leonardo Coello Escalante
Yizhe Wang
Matthew P. Erodici
Jonathon N. Nessralla
Ayana Tabo
Takashi Taniguchi
Kenji Watanabe
Ke Xu
David T. Limmer
D. Kwabena Bediako
item.description:
Authors: Sonal Maroo, Leonardo Coello Escalante, Yizhe Wang, et al.; last authors: David T. Limmer, D. Kwabena Bediako

Last/corresponding-author proxy: David T. Limmer, D. Kwabena Bediako

Score 6/10 B_IMPORTANT_CONDMAT

Why: Uses graphene electronic density of states to explain electron transfer; relevant for electronic structure.

Tags: #graphene #densityofstates #electronicstructure

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10311-2 By tuning graphene’s electronic density of states, the study shows electrode electronic structure—not just the electrolyte—dominates reorganization energy and thus controls outer-sphere electron-transfer rates at solid–liquid interfaces.

item.title: H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> repurposes plant O<sub>2</sub> sensing to regulate post-hypoxia responses
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10366-1

Experiments in Arabidopsis demonstrate that group VII ethylene response factors show differential responses to different stimuli, enabling plants in flood-prone environments to adapt to both submergence-induced hypoxia and reoxygenation upon desubmergence.
item.dc:title: H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> repurposes plant O<sub>2</sub> sensing to regulate post-hypoxia responses
item.dc:creator: Salma Akter, Monica Perri, Mikel Lavilla-Puerta, et al.; last authors: Emily Flashman, Francesco Licausi

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini response missing item; kept to avoid false negative

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10366-1 Experiments in Arabidopsis demonstrate that group VII ethylene response factors show differential responses to different stimuli, enabling plants in flood-prone environments to adapt to both submergence-induced hypoxia and reoxygenation upon desubmergence.

item.title: Symmetry classification of magnetic orders using oriented spin space groups
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10401-1

An oriented spin space group description is proposed that unifies the spin space group and magnetic space group frameworks, enabling a comprehensive symmetry classification of the magnetic materials on a large scale and identifying spin–orbit magnetism as a distinct phase.
item.dc:title: Symmetry classification of magnetic orders using oriented spin space groups
item.dc:creator: Yuntian Liu, Xiaobing Chen, Yutong Yu, Jesús Etxebarria, J. Manuel Perez-Mato, Qihang Liu
Xiaobing Chen
Yutong Yu
Jesús Etxebarria
J. Manuel Perez-Mato
Qihang Liu
item.description:
Authors: Yuntian Liu, Xiaobing Chen, Yutong Yu, Jesús Etxebarria, J. Manuel Perez-Mato, Qihang Liu

Last/corresponding-author proxy: J. Manuel Perez-Mato, Qihang Liu

Score 8/10 B_IMPORTANT_CONDMAT

Why: Theoretical framework for magnetic symmetry; directly relevant for altermagnetism and magnetic topological phases.

Tags: #altermagnetism #topologicalmaterials #magneticsymmetry

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10401-1 An oriented spin space group description is proposed that unifies the spin space group and magnetic space group frameworks, enabling a comprehensive symmetry classification of the magnetic materials on a large scale and identifying spin–orbit magnetism as a distinct phase.

item.title: Transposable elements are driving rapid adaptation of Enterococcus faecium
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10373-2

Over three decades, rapid expansion of the transposable element ISL3 has reshaped Enterococcus faecium, which helps to explain this pathogen’s growing clinical threat.
item.dc:title: Transposable elements are driving rapid adaptation of Enterococcus faecium
item.dc:creator: Matthew P. Grieshop, Aaron A. Behr, Sierra Bowden, et al.; last authors: Niaz Banaei, Ami S. Bhatt

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10373-2 Over three decades, rapid expansion of the transposable element ISL3 has reshaped Enterococcus faecium, which helps to explain this pathogen’s growing clinical threat.

item.title: Phonon Hall viscosity and the intrinsic thermal Hall effect of α-RuCl<sub>3</sub>
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10420-y

Ultrasonic measurements show phonon Hall viscosity drives an intrinsic thermal Hall effect in α-RuCl3, resolving its origin.
item.dc:title: Phonon Hall viscosity and the intrinsic thermal Hall effect of α-RuCl<sub>3</sub>
item.dc:creator: Avi Shragai, Ezekiel Horsley, Subin Kim, Young-June Kim, B. J. Ramshaw
Ezekiel Horsley
Subin Kim
Young-June Kim
B. J. Ramshaw
item.description:
Authors: Avi Shragai, Ezekiel Horsley, Subin Kim, Young-June Kim, B. J. Ramshaw

Last/corresponding-author proxy: Young-June Kim, B. J. Ramshaw

Score 5/10 C_MAYBE_UNCLASSIFIED

Why: Gemini response missing item; kept to avoid false negative

Tags: #phonon #Halleffect

Abstract: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10420-y Ultrasonic measurements show phonon Hall viscosity drives an intrinsic thermal Hall effect in α-RuCl3, resolving its origin.

item.title: Hybrid calculation of hadronic vacuum polarization in muon g − 2 to 0.48%
item.content:encoded:
Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10449-z

A new calculation for the anomalous magnetic moment of the muon using a hybrid approach combining experimental and lattice data in different energy ranges represents a validation of the standard model to 11 digits.
item.dc:title: Hybrid calculation of hadronic vacuum polarization in muon g − 2 to 0.48%
item.dc:creator: A. Boccaletti, Sz. Borsanyi, A. Cotellucci, et al.; last authors: G. Wang, Z. Zhang
